sentences.text: The-- what came almost naturally to me was that having secured a form of economic independence, it would have been logical for me to try and catch up on the things that I've missed out, in the war years and the-- and the conditions of early life in Australia, dictated by circumstances rather than by choice.
And with the help of my good wife, who has been very persuasive, I have applied-- I have applied to every university in Melbourne for acceptance.
I have produced my documentation of the graduation at the University of Warsaw, and so on.
I wrote to Melbourne University.
I never got a reply.
I went to Monash University, and they wouldn't look at me.
In desperation, I went to La Trobe.
And La Trobe wasn't much more interested, even though they had courses for mature-age students, already then.
They were the third university.
And I knew that if I will walk out from that visit to La Trobe, that would end of my dream.
So I decided to go and talk to people, from one office to another, in the administrative building, until I knocked at the door of a man, just one door away from the vice chancellor, Dr.
[INAUDIBLE].
I'm sure he's still alive.
Produced my papers, told him what I want to do, and I asked him to help me.
He looked at it, and he said, I've got no doubt in my mind that your-- the documents are genuine, that you have finished secondary education, that you have been to university.
But that was a long time ago.
How can you convince me that, almost 40 years later, you are still capable of studying?
How can one convince?
And you have to give an answer on the spot.
You can't-- you can't think too hard, too long.
And I was desperate.
And I turned around to him, and I said, I can only do one thing.
I've got personal friends in all the universities, at Melbourne.
And I gave him names of professors in law and in so on.
And I gave him names of professors at Monash.
And suddenly, it occurred to me.
Wait a minute, I said.
There is a professor right here at La Trobe who knows me.
His name is Professor [NON-ENGLISH].
Oh, he said.
You know Professor [NON-ENGLISH]?
I said yes, I know Professor [NON-ENGLISH], and Professor [NON-ENGLISH] knows me.
If you care to inquire about me from Professor [NON-ENGLISH], he'll be able to give you some sort of a opinion about me, or my capacity to study, or whatever you want to know from him.
He said, that's perfectly all right.
I won't have to ring him.
If you know Professor [NON-ENGLISH], Professor [NON-ENGLISH] knows you, you are in.
You are in, and wrote a note for acceptance, and then turned around to me and says, tell me.
With all the connections-- and I'm not mentioning all those names of the other professors, because I don't want to just mention names-- why didn't you use all those connections?
I said, it never occurred to me that I ought to get into a university by using connections of people who happen to know.
I want to get into university on the merits of my own ability or otherwise.
Anyhow, after one year, I finished up with-- I took three subjects there.
And every one of them asked me to do an honorary-- a honors course in his particular subject.
It was in history, and philosophy, and in the whatever else was the other-- the third subject.
But the history lecturer told me, don't stay here.
Take your results and go to Melbourne.
They will accept you now.
And so it was.
And so it was.
They grabbed me.
They saw my paper, they saw my results.
And I've done-- I've done an honors course at Melbourne and wrote a major work in-- in Jewish-- in Hebrew literature.
It was-- my work in the honors course was on the subject of the relative relevance of [NON-ENGLISH] to Jew in our time.
And it was a very well-received paper.

And when I finished it, I went-- at that time, Professor [NON-ENGLISH], if you remember that name-- he was a Japanese professor who came here, took over the Department of Middle Eastern Studies at Melbourne University-- just came and took over.
I came to see him.
He was a linguist.
He was not a professor of Judaica in the full sense, a gamut of all-- he was a linguist.
And I came, I said to him, I want to continue.
I've done my-- my honors degree.
I want to continue and do a master's.
He said, yes, do so.

And I suggest to you that I've-- that as you have written your honors thesis on the [NON-ENGLISH], and you came to such a conclusion-- and my conclusion was that [NON-ENGLISH] is, today, more relevant than [NON-ENGLISH] to Jew in our time.
He says, why don't you continue with that thought and develop that thought?
Go home and think of a subject, of a topic, and come back to me.
So I said to him, would you mind if I will throw out a subject right away?
I wasn't prepared for that suggestion.
But as you talk to me, would it be acceptable to you if I write my master's thesis on [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the philosophy of Zionism according to [NON-ENGLISH].
And he said, acceptable, and that was it, And-- So when did you complete your master's?
Within one year-- I went straight to Israel.
I couldn't-- I didn't have the search books here.
I went to the Hebrew University, spent six weeks there, very intensive study, and researched the subject, came back.
It took me-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] no, I can't remember now exactly how long.
And by the end of the year, submitted the master's, and it was sent away.
One copy went to England, to Manchester.
One copy went to Israel, to the very men who supervised my research, with-- unbeknown to me, the Melbourne department was connected with him.
So he knew me.
And the third one was assessed here And I had my master's.
And then I've published my autobiography.
Yes, in the meantime, Melbourne invited me to come and teach in the Hebrew literature, their department.
At first, they-- at first, I had my doubts.
But then I gave in, and I taught there for four years, from the age of 65 to 69.
And then, when I published my book, my autobiography--
I've taught from '80, '82, '83, '84, and '85.

When I published my book, my autobiography, which had a very good reception-- and you have seen one of the critiques-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Doctor Foster, who read my manuscript on his own-- on his own initiative talked to Professor [NON-ENGLISH], who was then a professor of economic-- the professor, the professor of economic history at Melbourne University, who, in turn, invited me to come and see him and suggested that I should write the history of the knitting industry in Victoria, as a doctoral thesis.
And I was-- and I say that with the benefit of hindsight-- a little bit [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] too easy in saying yes, because that-- I wanted to write a doctoral thesis.
But I wanted to continue in the field in which I have written my-- my-- the honors and my master's.

And I want to continue in this field, because that was the field which touched on the very essence of the relations between world Jew and the new State of Israel, which are so profound and so sensitive, and becoming more sensitive with time.
And for that, I would have had to go and interview people all over the world, and the personalities in America, and in Israel, and so on.
That's what I wanted to do.
But it did not-- it did not turn out this way.
There was an opportunity for me to-- to study at [INAUDIBLE].
We went to Boston, and my wife had one look at it, at the snow there.
And she said, you can stay here as long as you like.
I'm going back to Melbourne.
And that was the end of that idea.
So anyhow, I spent one and a half hours-- one half-- beg your pardon.

One and a half years with Professor [NON-ENGLISH], went out to do some field work for my-- for my thesis, and found that the amount of material was so skimpy, almost nonexistent, that it was-- it became obvious to me immediately that you can't write a doctorate on such skimpy material.

You can write an article in the paper about it, but you can't write a doctorate, and especially in economic history, which talks about economic figures, and tables, and relative developments, and so on.
It was-- there was nothing for me to handle, even though I knew-- I knew my history.
And I knew my basic economics.
And I-- the first year I've spent in-- in discussion with my group, and so on, and I held my own, even though they have had five years of economics and I'd had an arts subject.
But that was the end, regrettably, that I could-- I have never finished my doctorate in that subject.

But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] after-- after I've given that up, people who read my two essays-- the BA and the master's-- insisted, started to insist, that I should publish it, that I should-- that this sort of material ought to be published.
And until a couple of years ago, when Professor [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], who is an old friend of mine, came one day to me and said, can I have-- can I have your manuscript?
I want to read them.
I've heard about them.
And he came back after he read them and started abusing me.
He said, and you are sitting on such material and not publishing it?
He called me every name under the sun.
And he took the initiative to start pushing for some people to publish it.
And it's a long story.
I can't go into it.
The fact is that it's now going to be published by the Mandelbaum House, attached to the University of Sydney.
I was probably going, possibly, by the end of this month to Sydney, to finalize the conditions of the publication.
And it will, in all probability, be published by the middle of this year.
And it has been judged by the authorities in this field at Hebrew University, and at Oxford, and so on, as a suitable textbook for undergraduate and postgraduates in the English-speaking world.
And that's going to be published.
Yes, but-- but maybe not to the same extent as people who've-- who lived through it in camps.
For-- I-- I made sure that, on top of what they learned at school-- they went, both, to [NON-ENGLISH].
And of course, at [NON-ENGLISH] and at the time, when they had came there, when they arrived there, [NON-ENGLISH] already had a course in Jewish history.
And Shoah was-- the Holocaust was part of that course, and so on.

But of course, I've discussed with them and I've elaborated it, but not with-- from the perspective, the angle of depressing them, of-- of making them feel that-- that the vibrations of this tragedy are still touching them in a personal way.

I wanted them to understand it in the context of the totality of Jewish history, and understand it as a part of the imponderables of history generally, and the imponderables of the Jewish history, which had so many crises in its 5,000 years.
You may be surprised by my answer.
I was, all my life, never looking backwards.
I always looked forwards, in the sense that I didn't let whatever-- no matter how it ate my soul, how the tragedy has affected me-- all my family was wiped out.
The community was wiped out.
The people and-- but I was always looking at tomorrow from a positive-- the counter force, or the countervailing force, historically, was the establishment of the State of Israel.
It happened at-- almost at the same time.

And instead of thinking in terms of destruction, I thought in terms of building, instead in terms of creation, creation of the people, of the creation of a new future for our people, and creation of-- of a future for our community, a creation ensuring the existing continuity of new generations of Jews in Australia, which I could make some contribution and so on.
It was always a positive, constructive attitude, even if-- if I had to grapple with the tragedy on a personal basis.
But it was-- I didn't let that-- that destruction and that tragedy destroy my positive outlook on life and my faith in the future.

Well, maybe if you'll permit me to give you a-- a classical answer from the Bible-- the story of the parting of the Red Sea and the Exodus, as described in the Bible, yes-- where the waters parted and the Jewish people went through on dry land-- the commenter [NON-ENGLISH] makes a-- an extraordinary human observation about that act of-- of a supernatural act of parting of the sea.
[NON-ENGLISH] says that the sea did not part until the people went into the water, and the water reached right up to their mouths.
What-- that was allegorical, of course.
What he was trying to say is a very simple truism.
You have to go, yourself, as far as you can.
When you can't go any farther, then you're entitled to expect a miracle.
But if you stand at the edge of the water and wait until the miracle will occur, and then you will be brave enough to walk through it, then that miracle will never happen.
I have done all I could, according to my own understanding.
I ran, I didn't want to look back.
I didn't want to worry, what am I going to do in-- in Japan.
I didn't know there's going to be a committee there that will give me what to eat and a-- and a mattress to sleep on.
I ran because I knew that that is the-- it was by the singeing my-- my heels of my shoes, the war, so I ran.
And I knew that you were run-- run in the direction.
You ran away from the fire.
You ran away from the fire.
And the rest, you are entitled to circumstances, or a miracle, whatever it called, to survive it-- no guarantees.
I could have finished up going to a prison in Shanghai, with typhoid, and not come out alive.
There was no guarantee.
There was no guarantees about anything.
It so happened that because I have done everything that I could-- and you know [INAUDIBLE] puts it in the mouth of Tevye in the When you're lucky, the whole thinks that you are clever.
And luck-- luck is a major component in every human life.

I think about the Shoah because when one is trying to understand [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] anything about life, and one is trying to understand the-- the-- the unshakable belief of the Jewish people in the God of Israel and so on, one can't help thinking about the Shoah.
One can't help asking questions, fundamental questions about it.
And we all ask the same question.
Where was God in Auschwitz?
Where was God in Auschwitz?
Where was he?
Especially a religious person-- I-- I am not religious man, in the sense of orthodoxy, in a religious sense.
I am a man of-- of extraordinary-- a affinity of-- to the Jewish people, as a people.

My understanding and my philosophy-- when I have created Bialik as-- as a sequence to my involvement with Scopus-- and I'm making that point to make myself understood-- I've explained that I am creating Bialik because at Scopus, Jewish culture is part of Jewish religion.
At Bialik, Jewish religion is part of the Jewish culture.
And that is my affinity to my culture, to my people, to my past, to my future.
And that is-- that was the driving force within me, both ideologically, philosophically, in terms of an historical analysis and so on.
And it remains so, to this day.
And to that extent, one cannot-- how can one not think about-- one cannot-- you can't avoid.
But the Holocaust is a companion of ours, and not only of ours.
Any thinking man-- there may be other people who may be more detached.
But they can't avoid, if they are thinking and if they want to understand history in terms of a deeper perspective, or a more analytical retrospect.

Then one of the stumbling blocks for any thinking man must be the Holocaust, because you-- you have to come to terms about human nature, which we have always thought to be the-- the crown of God's creation.
And yet, it's turning out to be, at times, somewhat less than that.
Why did I want to tell my story?
Well, I told my story, first of all, because of private pressures of the family.
I told my story because it was-- the-- the experiences in Europe were widely, widely recorded.
Precisely because my-- my experiences were, in terms of tragedy, of a secondary nature-- I was the lucky one.
But historically, they ought to have-- to be recorded just the same, I thought, because it affected, not me.
It affected thousands of people, and to give some sort of equilibrium to the disequilibrium of-- of the Shoah in Europe, to the negation or overlooking of everything else.

And then, of course, I was mindful about my 40-years life and-- and activity in Australia, and the things that I-- I was fortunate enough to be able to create, and-- and I'm still doing, because as you know, I'm the chairman of the Committee for [NON-ENGLISH] Jewish Studies.
And we're now building university departments at Monash and at Melbourne, and maintaining, and so on.
I haven't finished yet.
I haven't finished yet.
As long as one is alive and one is-- half a brain is working, we are doing.
There's plenty to do, and not enough people to do it.
But there's still plenty, plenty to do, plenty.
